title: design specification for cross-invocation hexa-daemon (E1-E4 phasing)
headline: Daemon design is complete and implementable; HONEST verdict is DO-NOT-IMPLEMENT-NOW because no current workload uses HEXA_REMOTE_DOCKER=1 in batch — savings would be theoretical until a docker-mode batch workload appears. Design is preserved for the moment one does.

predecessors.omega_runner_13_phase_a: established bench harness (with /bin/true fixture bug, corrected in 13b)
predecessors.omega_runner_13b: proved persistent FIFO docker-exec achieves p50=1-3ms vs fresh ~80ms (25-50× speedup). Single-process, single-session FIFO POC.
predecessors.omega_runner_13c: audit-only close — bridge_health.sh runs hexa LOCALLY on host, not via docker exec; the 13b speedup applies only to docker-exec floor. Daemonization deferred to 13d as a dedicated cycle.

context.this_cycle_scope: DESIGN ONLY. Produce a complete spec a future ω-runner-13e can implement directly. No daemon code is written this cycle. The deliverable is this closure JSON plus an optional design-print .hexa for human browsing.

head_pinned.anima_pin: n/a (no commit in anima per PSC convention)

1_lifecycle.rationale: Always-on launchd KeepAlive reserves resources for workloads that may never run; lazy-spawn matches the actual usage pattern (sweeps run on demand, then idle). Idle-timeout (default 600s) reaps the daemon when unused.

start.trigger: client (hexa_remote or wrapper) checks for live socket; if dead/missing, attempts to acquire spawn lock and exec daemon binary
start.spawn_method: double-fork + setsid to detach from client TTY; client returns when socket accepts a PING (with 2s budget)
start.reaper: daemon traps SIGCHLD on its interp children; on parent exit, all children die

stop.idle_timeout: HEXA_DAEMON_IDLE_SECS (default 600). Daemon tracks last_request_ts; if now - last > idle, exit cleanly.
stop.explicit: client may send LINE `__SHUTDOWN__\n`; daemon flushes in-flight requests, closes socket, removes pid+sock+lock, exits.
stop.signal: SIGTERM → graceful (same as __SHUTDOWN__). SIGKILL → unclean; recovery handled by next client via stale-detection.

filesystem_layout.base_dir: $HOME/.hx/run/  (already exists on this host; consistent with hexa-lang convention)
filesystem_layout.lock_file: $HOME/.hx/run/hexa_daemon.lock  (flock LOCK_EX | LOCK_NB at spawn, held by daemon for life)

1_lifecycle.lock_rationale: Two clients racing the spawn must produce exactly one daemon. flock(LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB) on $HOME/.hx/run/hexa_daemon.lock — the loser falls back to retry-connect-with-backoff (3 tries × 200ms).

2_ipc_protocol.rationale_vs_alternatives: FIFO/named-pipe is multi-writer-unsafe and one-shot per open. TCP/localhost adds firewall+port-bind concerns and is reachable by other local users. Unix socket gives kernel-enforced same-host scope, file-perm authentication, and bidirectional bytestream — the cleanest match.
2_ipc_protocol.wire_format: length-prefixed JSON: 4-byte big-endian unsigned length, then UTF-8 JSON object. Bidirectional. Client sends one request frame, daemon sends one response frame, then connection closes. Connection-per-request keeps state-machine trivial.
2_ipc_protocol.rationale_vs_line_protocol: Line protocol (mirror of 13b) loses on (a) embedded newlines in stdin/stdout payloads requiring escape, (b) no clean way to multiplex stderr, (c) JSON gives free schema-evolution. 4-byte length prefix is 1ms cheaper than scanning for sentinel and avoids any in-band collision risk.

request_schema.id: uuid-or-monotonic-counter (echoed in response for client correlation)

control_ops.SHUTDOWN: request {op:SHUTDOWN}; daemon ACKs then exits after draining

2_ipc_protocol.framing_correctness_note: 4-byte length is unsigned big-endian; max frame size 16 MiB (hard cap, daemon rejects larger with error frame to prevent OOM).

3_recovery_health.client_side_timeout: every RUN has a per-request timeout_ms (default 30000). On timeout: client closes socket, marks daemon as suspect, falls back to fresh-exec for THIS request, records suspect-count.
3_recovery_health.suspect_threshold: after 3 consecutive timeouts within 60s, client treats socket as dead, removes stale pid/sock, attempts fresh spawn.
stale_detection: pid_file exists but kill(pid, 0) fails with ESRCH → stale → cleanup + respawn
socket exists but connect() fails with ECONNREFUSED → stale → cleanup + respawn
socket connect() succeeds but PING times out at 2s → suspect → fall back this call, mark for next-cycle cleanup
3_recovery_health.ping_pong: PING op exists but is NOT used as a periodic keepalive — clients PING once before each RUN only if last successful RUN was > 60s ago (avoids amortizing PING cost into the savings the daemon is supposed to provide).
3_recovery_health.restart_policy: manual on intentional shutdown (next client respawns lazily). NO launchd KeepAlive — see 1_lifecycle rationale.
3_recovery_health.crash_recovery_invariant: client RUN must NEVER hang indefinitely. Worst case: timeout fires → fresh-exec fallback → request still completes. Daemon failure is degradation, not denial.

4_auth_trust.primary_mechanism: Unix socket file mode 0600, owned by $UID, in directory $HOME/.hx/run/ also mode 0700.
4_auth_trust.kernel_enforced_invariant: On macOS+Linux, only the owning UID can connect() to a 0600 socket. Other local users get EACCES.
defense_in_depth: daemon calls getpeereid() (macOS) / SO_PEERCRED (Linux) on every accept(); if peer_uid != getuid() → close + log warn + drop request
directory $HOME/.hx/run/ pre-created with mode 0700 by daemon (mkdir + chmod), so even if root weakens the socket, the dir gates traversal
no shared-secret auth in v1 — would add complexity for marginal gain on single-user hosts; revisit in v2 if a multi-tenant target appears
4_auth_trust.threat_model_decision: Same-uid-on-same-host is the trust boundary. Anything inside is implicit-trust (matches existing hexa_remote model).

5_state_isolation.decision: Daemon is a request ORCHESTRATOR. Each RUN forks-execs a FRESH hexa-interp child. Daemon process itself never imports hexa-lang internals.
5_state_isolation.rationale: ω-runner-13c flagged interpreter-side state-leak as the open question for in-process amortization. Forking a fresh interp per request eliminates that risk entirely AT THE COST of losing the ~35ms host-side hexa-startup amortization. The remaining savings come from docker-exec floor amortization (when used) and elimination of the per-call hexa_remote overhead (resolver, env-prep, ssh handshake if applicable).
5_state_isolation.implication: Daemon savings = (per-call docker-exec floor savings via persistent docker-exec coproc inside daemon) + (per-call hexa_remote setup elimination). NOT (host-side hexa-interp startup savings). This narrows the candidate workloads — see section 9.
5_state_isolation.alternative_rejected: in-process hexa eval with module-system reset hook: requires hexa-lang change (out of nexus scope), high engineering cost, and one missed reset hook silently corrupts cross-request state. Fork-per-request is the safe default and is what 13c implicitly recommended.
5_state_isolation.docker_exec_amortization_inside_daemon: Daemon at startup spawns ONE persistent `docker exec -i $C sh` coproc per configured remote target (using 13b's FIFO pattern). For RUN ops whose resolved transport is docker-exec, daemon multiplexes through the existing coproc. Cross-target requests use distinct coprocs.

6_integration_contract.client_detection: single env var: HEXA_DAEMON_SOCK. If set and non-empty AND ping-ok → route through socket. Else → existing fresh-exec path. Operator-toggleable without modifying hexa_remote code.
6_integration_contract.auto_discovery: if HEXA_DAEMON_SOCK is unset BUT $HOME/.hx/run/hexa_daemon.sock exists AND is a socket AND PING returns ok within 200ms → use it (opportunistic). This is a minor convenience and CAN be disabled by HEXA_DAEMON_SOCK=NONE.
6_integration_contract.hexa_remote_patch_scope: minimal: detect socket + length-prefixed JSON request + length-prefixed JSON response. ~30-50 LoC near L1228 (the existing HEXA_REMOTE_DOCKER branch). Falls back unchanged if socket absent.
6_integration_contract.wrapper_hexa_remote_relation: scripts/bin/hexa_remote remains the canonical client; daemon is invisible to scripts that already use hexa_remote. Scripts calling `$HEXA_BIN run` directly (like bridge_health.sh L248) bypass the daemon — by design, since 13c showed bridge_health.sh's bottleneck is host-local startup, not docker-exec floor.

in_scope.compromised_socket_or_dir_perms: mitigated by mode 0700 dir + 0600 sock + getpeereid() check
in_scope.stale_pid_or_socket_after_crash: mitigated by kill(pid,0) probe + connect-with-2s-PING + cleanup-and-respawn under flock
in_scope.race_on_spawn: mitigated by flock LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB on lock_file; loser retries connect

accepted_residual.compromised_daemon_process: if attacker code runs as $UID and ptrace-attaches the daemon, all subsequent requests are exposed. Mitigation: same trust domain as the user's shell; equivalent to attacker already owning the user account.
accepted_residual.compromised_hexa_binary: out of scope; same as fresh-exec model

explicitly_out_of_scope: multi-tenant hosts (would need shared-secret auth + per-tenant socket dirs)
container escape via daemon (daemon never runs in privileged container; if user opts to run daemon inside container, they accept that boundary)
side-channel timing attacks across requests (no hardening; same as fresh-exec)

8_failure_modes.recover: client falls back to fresh-exec for current request; if 3 timeouts in 60s, force-cleanup + respawn

8_failure_modes.detect: daemon's accept-time getpeereid() rejects non-self uid
8_failure_modes.recover: log warn; drop connection; alert via stderr
daemon sends error frame {error: 'frame_too_large'}; closes connection; no state corruption
daemon SIGKILLs child; returns {exit_code: -1, error: 'timeout'} to client
daemon swallows log error; serves requests (logs are best-effort)
daemon writes warning to log; this is operator misconfiguration, not a daemon-level recovery target

daemon_overhead_per_request.fork_exec_hexa_interp_child: ~35 ms (the host-side hexa startup IS still paid because we fork-per-request; see section 5)

9_performance_ceiling_reestimate.savings_only_realize_when: transport floor > daemon overhead. Concretely: docker-exec mode (floor ~80ms → savings ~75ms via persistent coproc) — net win. Pure local hexa run (floor ~35ms → savings 0, since fork-per-request still pays it) — NET LOSS of ~3-5ms per call.

candidate_workloads_evaluated.name: bridge_health.sh sweep (16 bridges, host-local hexa)
candidate_workloads_evaluated.verdict: DAEMON DOES NOT HELP — fork-per-request still pays 35ms × 16, daemon adds 3-5ms overhead per call. Use `hexa batch` (when implemented in hexa-lang) instead.

candidate_workloads_evaluated.name: hypothetical bridge_health.sh sweep with HEXA_REMOTE_DOCKER=1 routed via daemon
candidate_workloads_evaluated.verdict: WOULD BENEFIT (16 × ~75ms = ~1.2s saved per sweep). But this routing does not exist today.

candidate_workloads_evaluated.name: interactive REPL-style usage (developer running hexa run X repeatedly)
candidate_workloads_evaluated.verdict: MARGINAL — saves 35ms hexa_remote overhead per call, but interactive cadence is human-paced. Negligible.

candidate_workloads_evaluated.name: future CI workload running 100+ hexa runs in docker-exec mode

honest_verdict_on_implementation.primary_reasoning: No current workload uses HEXA_REMOTE_DOCKER=1 in batch. The dominant nexus workload (bridge_health.sh) runs hexa locally and would NOT benefit (in fact would regress slightly). 13b's 25-50× speedup is real but is locked behind 'all calls go through docker exec'. Until a docker-mode batch workload appears, this daemon is a solution looking for a problem.
honest_verdict_on_implementation.secondary_reasoning: Operational burden is non-trivial — pid+lock+sock lifecycle, recovery, peer-uid checks, framing — all for ZERO measured benefit on the current workload mix.
honest_verdict_on_implementation.what_changes_the_calculus: (a) bridge_health.sh sweep is rerouted through hexa_remote in HEXA_REMOTE_DOCKER=1 mode, OR (b) a new CI workload spawns ≥50 docker-exec hexa runs per sweep. EITHER condition flips this from theoretical to ~10-50× win.
honest_verdict_on_implementation.shelf_value_of_this_design: the design is pre-baked, frozen, reviewable. When a triggering workload appears, the implementer's day-zero is not 'design from scratch' — it is 'execute E1-E4 below'. Estimated implementation cost dropped from ~3 cycles (with design+impl) to ~1.5 cycles (impl only).

E1_minimal_daemon.goal: prove socket+spawn+request wiring end-to-end on a single workload
scope: single client at a time (no multi-client mux)
Unix socket at $HOME/.hx/run/hexa_daemon.sock
length-prefixed JSON RUN op only (no PING, no STATUS, no SHUTDOWN — daemon stays up forever in E1)
fork-exec hexa-interp per request
no pid file, no lock file, no recovery, no auth (mode 0600 set but no peer-uid check yet)
no idle timeout — daemon runs until SIGTERM
E1_minimal_daemon.deliverable: tool/hexa_daemon.hexa (or scripts/bin/hexa_daemon shell wrapper if hexa-lang lacks socket primitives)
E1_minimal_daemon.validation: client spawns daemon, sends 100 RUN reqs to `hexa run tool/runner_exec_bench.hexa --runs 1`, all return exit_code 0; wall-time matches direct fork-exec within ±5ms
E1_minimal_daemon.exit_criteria: demonstrably-routed request, no state leak across requests (each request gets its own child)

scope: pid file write+read; flock on lock file
stale-pid detection (kill(pid,0) ESRCH → cleanup)
stale-socket detection (connect ECONNREFUSED → cleanup)
per-request timeout_ms in daemon; SIGKILL child on timeout
client-side fresh-exec fallback on connect failure or PING timeout
PING + STATUS + SHUTDOWN ops added
E2_lock_pid_recovery.validation: kill -9 daemon mid-request → next client request still completes (via respawn or fallback); two-client race spawns exactly one daemon

E3_launchd_optional.goal: optional always-on mode for users who want it (NOT default)
scope: $HOME/Library/LaunchAgents/com.hexa.daemon.plist generator
RunAtLoad: false; KeepAlive: SuccessfulExit: false (only restart on crash)
operator opt-in: `hexa daemon install` / `hexa daemon uninstall` subcommands
lazy-spawn remains the default; launchd is for users with constant docker-exec batch traffic
E3_launchd_optional.validation: install + reboot + first hexa_remote call uses daemon without spawn

E4_hexa_remote_wiring.goal: actually route hexa_remote requests through daemon when HEXA_DAEMON_SOCK is set or auto-discovered
scope: scripts/bin/hexa_remote: detect HEXA_DAEMON_SOCK, framed JSON request, framed JSON response
fallback to existing fresh-exec path on any daemon-side error
respect HEXA_DAEMON_SOCK=NONE escape hatch
auto-discovery only if PING completes within 200ms
E4_hexa_remote_wiring.blockers: scripts/bin/hexa_remote is currently in parallel-session collision set per ω-runner-13d brief; E4 must NOT proceed until that file is no longer locked across sessions
E4_hexa_remote_wiring.validation: with daemon up: 16-bridge HEXA_REMOTE_DOCKER=1 sweep wall-time drops by ≥10× vs daemon-off baseline
E4_hexa_remote_wiring.estimated_loc: +30-50 in hexa_remote; +20 in any wrapper scripts that opt in

five_bullet_summary: lifecycle: lazy-spawn-on-first-request; idle-timeout 600s; pid+lock+sock under $HOME/.hx/run/; double-fork+setsid; flock LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB to prevent race-spawn
ipc protocol: Unix domain SOCK_STREAM at $HOME/.hx/run/hexa_daemon.sock with 4-byte big-endian length prefix + JSON v1 frame; RUN/PING/STATUS/SHUTDOWN ops; connection-per-request
auth: socket mode 0600 + dir 0700 + accept-time getpeereid()/SO_PEERCRED self-uid check (defense in depth); single-user trust domain; no shared-secret in v1

state isolation: daemon is a request orchestrator; each RUN forks a fresh hexa-interp child; savings come ONLY from docker-exec floor amortization (per-target persistent coproc inside daemon), NOT from host-side hexa startup

integration: HEXA_DAEMON_SOCK env var toggles routing; auto-discovery with 200ms PING budget; HEXA_DAEMON_SOCK=NONE forces fresh-exec; minimal patch in hexa_remote near L1228 (deferred until that file leaves parallel-session lock)

honest_verdict.reason: no current workload uses HEXA_REMOTE_DOCKER=1 in batch. Dominant workload (bridge_health.sh) runs hexa locally and would slightly regress. The 13b 25-50× speedup is locked behind a transport (docker-exec floor) that no batch workload currently uses.
honest_verdict.trigger_to_revisit: (a) bridge_health.sh sweep is rerouted through HEXA_REMOTE_DOCKER=1, OR (b) a new ≥50-call docker-mode batch workload appears in CI
honest_verdict.shelf_value: design is frozen — when a trigger fires, implementer skips ~1.5 cycles of design work and goes straight to E1

files_created.reason: anima closure JSONs are untracked per PSC convention
design-print tool — prints the 5-bullet summary + verdict for human browsing; no executable daemon code; raw 9 .hexa-only-suffix compliant; raw 71 report-only

scope_constraints_honoured: no edit to parallel-session-locked files: cli/run.hexa, state/atlas_health_timeline.jsonl, bisociation/spectra/g_atlas_composite_v3.json, tool/omega_cycle_atlas_ingest.hexa, n6/*, tool/kick_*.hexa, design/kick/*, .own, scripts/bin/hexa_remote — none touched
hexa-only-suffix (raw 9): only one new file in nexus, tool/hexa_daemon_design.hexa (.hexa suffix, sweeper-safe)
raw 71 report-only: design-print emits to stdout, writes nothing to disk
no commit in anima — closure JSON written as untracked
explicit-path commit only — `git add tool/hexa_daemon_design.hexa` (no -A, no .)
history op safety: HEAD pinned to 423823514908e1639b248acfe300ecb5b566d699, no HEAD~N references
CLI-only inter-call: design-print is a pure stdout tool
next_followups: ω-runner-13e (CONDITIONAL on a triggering workload appearing): execute E1-E4 from this spec directly. Estimated ~1.5 cycles given design is pre-frozen.
ω-hexa-batch (independent track): if `hexa batch` ever lands in hexa-lang, bridge_health.sh sweep can be amortized WITHOUT a daemon — re-evaluate which path is actually shipped.
monitor for the trigger conditions: bridge_health.sh sweep ever rerouted through HEXA_REMOTE_DOCKER=1; OR a new ≥50-call docker-mode batch workload in CI
if/when scripts/bin/hexa_remote leaves parallel-session lock, the E4 patch surface is the L1228 docker-exec branch — note that for the cycle that picks this up
honesty_note: ω-runner-13c flagged daemon as 'non-trivial and out of scope for one cycle' — that was correct. ω-runner-13d's role is to make that statement REVERSIBLE: the next cycle can flip from 'out of scope' to 'execute E1-E4' with the design already done. The honest verdict on implementation is still NO at this moment because the workload doesn't justify it; but the option is now cheap to exercise.
